COO Version 

Hi {{Name}}, Barry-John here from CWSI.
We worked with Ryanair to simplify EFB ownership, which removed 15kg per flight and preserved about £700k per year by avoiding extra support headcount.

How is EFB ownership structured on your side?

Objections 

1- “We already have a partner.”

Understood.
Has that model stayed flat in terms of internal resource?

2- “It’s handled internally.”

From your perspective, is it fully optimised, or just functioning?

3- “We’re not changing anything.”

Fair enough.
Has the manual layer disappeared completely, or does some of it still sit in ops

5- “Send me something.”

Happy to.
What would be more relevant, the headcount angle or the operational continuity side?

Pushback To Remember

If this is already fully optimised on your side, I will not waste your time. But if you doubled fleet size tomorrow, would it scale without adding resource?

5 Questions 

- How is ownership structured today?
- Has headcount stayed flat?
- Where does the manual work still sit?
- Would it scale cleanly?
- Out of curiosity, have you ever put a number against the manual overhead sitting around EFB ownership today?

Head of Flight Ops Version

Hi {{Name}}, Barry-John here from CWSI and the reason for my call is because we worked with Ryanair on centralising EFB ownership so pilots always had the right information and Ops wasn’t absorbing device friction or update issues.

How are EFB updates and device ownership handled on your side?


Head of Flight Ops - Objections
- "IT handles that."
Got it. When something around EFBs goes wrong, does it stay in IT or does some of it land in Ops?

- "It works fine."

That’s good. Do pilots or cabin crew ever run into version or device issues at all?

- "We’re too small for that."

Fair point. In smaller fleets it usually shows up as one or two people quietly absorbing extra work. Has that happened?

- "We’re happy with the setup."

Understood. If you were designing it from scratch today, would it look exactly the same?

If Engaged Pick one of these questions:

- Do pilots ever run into version or device issues?
- Who steps in when something doesn’t go as planned?
- How much manual checking still sits around the process?
- Does IT own it fully, or does some of it land in Ops?

Bail-Out (Flight Ops)
If it’s already clean and stable on your side, I won’t waste your time. Out of curiosity, do your teams ever have to step in when something around EFBs doesn’t go smoothly?

CTA:

Would you be open to jumping on an exploratory call?
Send Intro SMS to increase chances of answering

Send voice note elevator pitch:

Hi {{first_name}},


At Ryanair, removing paper took about 15kg per flight out of the aircraft and delivered roughly £600k a year in savings. This became £700k by simplifying ownership and avoiding adding additional headcount.


The bigger insight was that manual work genuinely disappeared rather than shifting teams. 


I'm wondering whether that dynamic exists in your setup and if a quick sync / note sharing would be worthwhile?

Hi ,


Barry-John here from CWSI. Quick reason for the call:


- we're helping similar UK healthcare organisations prepare their Microsoft environment before rolling out Copilot.


- We're seeing a lot of teams excited about AI- but who are lacking clear visibility of over-permissioned data, unlabelled patient records, and shadow AI usage.


- We run a short readiness assessment that surfaces those issues safely and helps implement secure foundations so that Copilot can be deployed with confidence and minimal disruption. 


How are you currently approaching Copilot readiness internally?


Questions:


(1) Have you already rolled out Copilot or are you still planning it?


(2) How confident are you in your data visibility before enabling Copilot?


(3) Have you reviewed permissions and sensitive data exposure recently?


(4) How are you validating governance before AI rollout?


(5) If Copilot surfaced something sensitive tomorrow, how ready would you be?

SMS

Hi {{first_name}}, 

CWSI are a 4 x Microsoft Partner of the Year and we help organisations validate data visibility before rolling out Copilot. We recently uncovered over-permissioned patient data with while doing an assessment with another healthcare provider and this compelled me to reach out.

Are you already live with Copilot or still preparing? If the latter, would a quick exploratory chat make sense?

Hi [Name] - I’ll be brief.


• CWSI works with large multi-practice veterinary groups across Europe
• We typically see risk sitting at branch level - shared reception logins, rotating staff, laptops moving between consult rooms
• Client, payment and insurance data often accessed in the same session
• We help tighten that up using Microsoft-native controls - without slowing practices down



Just out of curiosity - how confident are you that a compromised practice device couldn’t spread across branches?


Open Ended Questions 



“How are you currently managing shared devices and rotating staff access across practices?”


“If a branch laptop was compromised tomorrow, what would containment look like in practice?”


“How do you ensure security policies are applied consistently across every practice — not just centrally?”


“Is strengthening practice-level security something you’re actively reviewing this year, or is it more reactive today?”

Hi {{FirstName}}, Barry from CWSI.


You may have seen I sent you a quick email.
Just to be clear — I’m not calling to sell you anything.
I noticed Ricoh has around 14,000 users on Microsoft E3 with a renewal coming up in July, and organisations of that size are currently eligible for Microsoft-funded security and Copilot readiness engagements.


We’re helping companies review their Microsoft security posture and identify gaps — fully funded by Microsoft.
So I just wanted to see  if that’s something already on your radar.


Receptive:

Typically these workshops help teams:
• review identity and threat protection
• identify security gaps in the M365 estate
• explore what additional protection E5 unlocks
• prepare the environment for Copilot safely



No cost — Microsoft funds the engagement.
Not Receptive:
No problem at all.
I only reached out because Ricoh qualifies due to the size of the environment, and many organisations are taking advantage of it ahead of renewals.


Would it make sense for me to send over the details and you can decide if it's worth exploring?
